welcomeMessage.en: Today, we are diving into the world of entertainment!
welcomeMessage.zh: 今天，我们将深入探讨娱乐世界！

welcomeMessage.en: We will talk about our favorite movies, TV shows, and how to describe a great story.
welcomeMessage.zh: 我们将谈论我们最喜欢的电影、电视节目以及如何描述一个好故事。

welcomeMessage.en: Grab your popcorn and get ready to share your cinematic masterpieces.
welcomeMessage.zh: 拿好你的爆米花，准备分享你心中的电影杰作。

questionsToThinkAbout.en: What is the most addictive TV show you have ever binge-watched?
questionsToThinkAbout.zh: 你曾经疯狂刷过的最让人上瘾的电视剧是什么？

questionsToThinkAbout.en: If you could pitch an original movie plot to a famous director, what would the story be about?
questionsToThinkAbout.zh: 如果你能向著名导演推销一个原创电影情节，故事会是关于什么的？

questionsToThinkAbout.en: Would you rather take a relaxing vacation on a cruise ship, or an exciting trip on a sci-fi star ship?
questionsToThinkAbout.zh: 你宁愿在游轮上度过一个轻松的假期，还是在科幻星际飞船上来一次刺激的旅行？

questionsToThinkAbout.en: How do you feel when someone gives you a spoiler for a movie you really wanted to see?
questionsToThinkAbout.zh: 当有人给你非常想看的电影剧透时，你有什么感觉？

questionsToThinkAbout.en: If you were immortal, how would you spend your unlimited free time?
questionsToThinkAbout.zh: 如果你是长生不老的，你会如何度过你无限的空闲时间？

vocabulary.definition: The main events of a play, novel, movie, or similar work.
vocabulary.example: The movie's special effects were great, but the plot was a little bit boring.

vocabulary.definition: Providing, involving, or characterized by deep absorption or immersion in something.
vocabulary.example: Watching an action movie in an IMAX theater is a very immersive experience.

vocabulary.definition: An event or occurrence which leaves an impression on someone.
vocabulary.example: Traveling to a new country is always an unforgettable experience.

vocabulary.definition: A strong feeling deriving from one's circumstances, mood, or relationships with others.
vocabulary.example: The actors showed so much emotion during the sad scenes.

vocabulary.definition: Present or existing from the beginning; first or earliest.
vocabulary.example: I prefer to watch movies with the original English audio rather than dubbed versions.

vocabulary.definition: Sound, especially when recorded, transmitted, or reproduced.
vocabulary.example: The theater has a great surround sound audio system.

vocabulary.definition: Watch multiple episodes of a television program in rapid succession.
vocabulary.example: On rainy weekends, I love to stay home and binge-watch my favorite TV series.

vocabulary.definition: Causing or likely to cause someone to become addicted to it.
vocabulary.example: That new puzzle game on my phone is so addictive; I can't stop playing it.

vocabulary.definition: A large ship like a hotel, that people travel on for pleasure.
vocabulary.example: Taking a vacation on a luxury cruise ship is very relaxing.

vocabulary.definition: A large floating mass of ice detached from a glacier or ice sheet.
vocabulary.example: The Titanic sank because it hit a giant iceberg in the dark.

vocabulary.definition: A description of an important plot development which may reduce surprise for a viewer.
vocabulary.example: Please don't tell me the ending! I hate spoilers!

vocabulary.definition: Break free from confinement or control.
vocabulary.example: In the movie, the hero has to escape from the bad guys.

vocabulary.definition: Burst or shatter violently and noisily as a result of rapid combustion.
vocabulary.example: Action movies always have scenes where cars or buildings explode.

vocabulary.definition: A spacecraft designed for interstellar travel.
vocabulary.example: The captain steered the star ship to another galaxy.

vocabulary.definition: Living forever; never dying or decaying.
vocabulary.translation: 长生不老的 / 不朽的
vocabulary.example: Vampires are immortal; they can live for thousands of years.

vocabulary.definition: Make a bid to obtain a contract or other business by presenting a proposal.
vocabulary.example: The director had to pitch his movie idea to the studio to get money.

sessionSummary: Today's class was a blockbuster! We dove into the world of entertainment, discussing our favorite movies, addictive TV series, and how to describe a great story.

We talked about classic films like Titanic, discussing the tragic plot involving the massive cruise ship and the iceberg. We also explored exciting sci-fi movies, picturing giant star ships exploding as the heroes try to escape.
Yating and Kiki shared how easy it is to binge-watch a really addictive show, especially when the original plot is so good and the audio makes it an immersive experience that brings out real emotion.

Peter had some great ideas and even learned how to 'pitch' an original movie concept about an immortal character. Most importantly, we all agreed on the golden rule of discussing movies: never give away a spoiler!

keyVocabulary.example: "The movie's special effects were great, but the plot was a little bit boring."
"Watching an action movie in an IMAX theater is a very immersive experience."
"Traveling to a new country is always an unforgettable experience."
"The actors showed so much emotion during the sad scenes."
"I prefer to watch movies with the original English audio rather than dubbed versions."
"The theater has a great surround sound audio system."
"On rainy weekends, I love to stay home and binge-watch my favorite TV series."
"That new puzzle game on my phone is so addictive; I can't stop playing it."
"Taking a vacation on a luxury cruise ship is very relaxing."
"The Titanic sank because it hit a giant iceberg in the dark."
"Please don't tell me the ending! I hate spoilers!"
"In the movie, the hero has to escape from the bad guys."
"Action movies always have scenes where cars or buildings explode."
"The captain steered the star ship to another galaxy."

keyVocabulary.translation: 长生不老的 / 不朽的
keyVocabulary.example: "Vampires are immortal; they can live for thousands of years."
"The director had to pitch his movie idea to the studio to get money."

grammarTips.title: Using 'Bored' vs. 'Boring'
grammarTips.description: When talking about movies, remember the 'ED/ING' rule. Use '-ed' for how YOU feel. Use '-ing' for the THING that causes the feeling.
grammarTips.incorrect: "I am boring because the movie is bored."
grammarTips.correct: "I am BORED because the movie is BORING."

grammarTips.title: How to use 'Spoiler'
grammarTips.description: In everyday conversation, if someone is about to talk about a movie you haven't seen, you can politely stop them with a common phrase.
grammarTips.correct: "No spoilers, please! / Don't give me any spoilers!"

grammarTips.title: Binge-watching
grammarTips.description: The word 'binge' means to do something excessively in a short period. It is most commonly used with TV shows now.
grammarTips.incorrect: "I watched the TV series for 10 hours non-stop."
grammarTips.correct: "I binge-watched the entire TV series in one day."

grammarTips.title: Pitching an idea
grammarTips.description: The word 'pitch' has many meanings (like a baseball pitch), but in business or storytelling, it means to quickly and excitedly explain an idea to persuade someone.
grammarTips.incorrect: "Let me say my idea to you."
grammarTips.correct: "Let me pitch my idea to you."

goals: Ensure students understand the difference between -ed and -ing adjectives (bored vs. boring, excited vs. exciting).
Encourage students to use vocabulary like 'plot' and 'spoiler' naturally when talking about storytelling.
Help students construct a narrative when 'pitching' a story, ensuring events follow a logical sequence.

phases.title: Phase 1: General Movie Talk (15-20 Minutes)
phases.description: Warm-up with questions about favorite genres, binge-watching habits, and recent movies.
questions: What's a movie or show you found extremely addictive? Did you binge-watch it?
Are you someone who likes spoilers before seeing a movie?

phases.title: Phase 2: Explaining a Plot (20-30 Minutes)
phases.description: Have students describe the plot of a favorite movie without giving away the ending.
questions: Describe a scene that was very immersive or emotional.
Who was the main character, and what were they trying to escape from or accomplish?

phases.title: Phase 3: Pitch Your Movie (15-20 Minutes)
phases.description: Students take turns pitching an original movie idea using new vocabulary like 'pitch', 'immortal', 'star ship', etc.
questions: Tell me your elevator pitch: What is the movie about, and why will people want to watch it?
